title: While Loops
description: Loop infinitely as long as a condition holds true.

slides.title: When you don't know the end 🤔
slides.content:
## Continuous Loops

A `for` loop is great when you know exactly how many times you want to iterate (e.g., 1 to 10).

But what if you want a program to keep asking the user for a password until they get it right? You don't know if it will take 1 try or 100 tries.

slides.title: The While Loop 🔁
slides.content:
A `while` loop checks a condition. If it is `true`, it runs the block of code inside `{}`.

Once the block finishes, it jumps back to the top and checks the condition again. It keeps doing this until the condition becomes `false`.

quizData.question: When does a `while` loop decide to stop executing its code block?

options.text: When the compiler tells it to.
The moment its condition evaluates to false at the start of a cycle.

quizData.explanation: The loop evaluates the condition every single cycle. If it's false, the loop instantly breaks.

slides.title: The Infinite Danger ☠️
slides.content:
## Infinite Loops

You MUST ensure that something inside the `while` block eventually makes the condition evaluate to `false`.

If you don't (for instance, if you forget to write `count--` in the previous example), the loop will run forever, freezing your application and draining the user's battery!

quizData.question: What is the result of running this code? `var x = 1; while(x > 0) { println(x) }`

options.text: It creates an infinite loop because `x` never decreases to 0 or below.

quizData.explanation: Because x remains 1 forever, the condition `x > 0` remains true forever. This creates an infinite loop that crashes the app.
